behaviors.description: Cache keys generated from identical query parameters are always identical, and keys from different parameters never collide.
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic key generation logic. The failure is in the hash/normalization algorithm itself. Given the same inputs, it must always produce the same output; given different inputs, it must produce different outputs. A pure function test with various parameter combinations (identical, slightly different, edge cases like special characters, null values) catches all real failures without needing caching infrastructure, database, or OS-level concerns.
behaviors.test_description: Call the key generation function with parametrized inputs: (1) identical parameter sets to verify determinism, (2) systematically varied parameters to verify uniqueness, (3) edge cases (Unicode, special chars, empty values) to catch encoding bugs. Assert no collisions and deterministic output.

behaviors.description: The TTL for a cache entry is computed correctly based on the data category's configured freshness rule (e.g., user profiles = 5 minutes, product catalog = 1 hour).
behaviors.justification: TTL computation is a pure function: given a category and a freshness configuration, it must return the correct time value. The failure is in the calculation logic (incorrect formula, wrong constant, misread config). This can be tested by injecting configuration and category as function arguments, without needing to load configuration from files or test the full caching pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Pass various (category, freshness_rule_config) pairs to the TTL calculation function and verify the returned TTL matches expected values. Test different data types (user profiles, product catalog, etc.) with their configured rules. Include edge cases like zero TTL or infinite TTL.

behaviors.description: When memory usage exceeds the configured threshold, the eviction strategy switches from LRU to LFU as specified in the configuration.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires observing cache behavior under memory pressure. A pure logic test with mocked memory checks would miss real failures in how the cache calculates its own memory usage or how the strategy-switching integrates with cache operations. The real failure could be: (1) strategy-switching logic is wrong (Unit catches), or (2) memory pressure isn't correctly detected in the actual cache implementation (Integration/System catches). Integration level—actually filling a cache and triggering memory-pressure conditions—catches both. Mocking memory without actually exercising the cache would hide real integration bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Create a cache with LRU strategy and set a low memory threshold. Incrementally add items until memory usage exceeds the threshold. Verify the eviction strategy changes to LFU. Test with multiple thresholds and verify the switch is idempotent (switching twice doesn't cause errors).

behaviors.description: A write to the underlying PostgreSQL table invalidates all cache entries whose keys match the affected rows.
behaviors.justification: This behavior spans two systems: the cache and the database. The real failure is not just in the invalidation logic, but in whether invalidation is actually triggered when the database is written. If invalidation is triggered by a database trigger, ORM hook, or event listener, we must write to an actual database to test it. A unit test that mocks DB writes at the ORM level would miss failures in the database-side trigger mechanism, event subscription, or timing. Integration level with a local test database catches the full flow.
behaviors.test_description: Use a test PostgreSQL database. Insert/update/delete rows in a table that has cached entries. Verify that cache keys corresponding to the affected rows are invalidated. Test multiple operations in sequence and verify only relevant cache entries are invalidated (not over-invalidation). Verify invalidation happens synchronously or with acceptable latency.

behaviors.description: The eviction policy removes the correct entries — least-recently-used under LRU, least-frequently-used under LFU — when the cache is full.
behaviors.justification: This is pure eviction algorithm logic. Given a cache state with known access patterns (timestamps for LRU, frequencies for LFU), the algorithm must return the correct entry to evict. The failure is in the comparison or data structure (e.g., wrong comparison operator, forgot to track frequency). This can be tested entirely in-process by creating a cache object, controlling access patterns programmatically, and verifying which entry is selected for eviction.
behaviors.test_description: Create in-memory cache objects with LRU and LFU strategies. Add entries with known access timestamps/frequencies. Fill cache beyond capacity. Verify eviction removes the least-recently-used entry (LRU) or least-frequently-used entry (LFU) respectively. Test tie-breaking (when multiple entries have same recency/frequency).
